params.text:
Hazardous substances in a care setting

You probably handle several hazardous substances during a normal shift without thinking of them as "hazardous". Recognising them is the first step to using them safely.

  • Cleaning products – multi-surface cleaners, oven cleaners, descalers and toilet cleaners, some of which are strongly acidic or alkaline.
  • Disinfectants and bleach – powerful at killing germs but corrosive and irritant; never mix bleach with other cleaners as this can release toxic gas.
  • Clinical and hazardous waste – used dressings, sharps and continence products that may carry infection.
  • Bodily fluids – blood, urine, faeces, vomit and sputum, which are biological hazards that can spread infection.
  • Some medicines – certain creams, cytotoxic drugs and crushed or handled tablets can be absorbed through the skin.
  • Dust and fine particles – from dry sweeping, talcum powder or building work, which can be breathed in.

params.question: Which of the following would be considered hazardous substances under COSHH in a care home? (Select all that apply.)

answers.text: Thick bleach used to disinfect a bathroom

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Yes. Bleach is corrosive and an irritant, and is a hazardous substance under COSHH.
Yes. Bodily fluids are biological hazards that can carry infection and are covered by COSHH.
Yes. Inhaling dust can harm the lungs, so it is a hazardous substance under COSHH.

answers.text: A sealed carton of long-life milk in the kitchen

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Ordinary food such as milk is not a COSHH hazardous substance.
